normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app secures sensitive operations with additional authentication.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-AUTH-3 (Description): Some additional form of authentication is often desirable for sensitive actions inside the app. This can be done in different ways (biometric, pin, MFA code generator, email, deep links, etc) and they all need to be implemented securely.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must require additional authentication beyond the initial session login for sensitive operations such as payment authorization, account changes, data export, privileged actions, and access to high-value key material. The step-up mechanism (biometric re-prompt, PIN re-entry, MFA code, email confirmation, signed deep link) must be implemented securely — bound to the sensitive operation, resistant to replay, and not bypassable via UI navigation or client-side authorization flags alone.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies to every app that declares an authentication flow (technical.auth_flow != "none") AND performs sensitive operations beyond initial session login. Per Constitution Pillar 4 (Authentication & Identity), step-up authentication activates at Tier 3+ where the app handles user authentication and private user-to-user content; sensitive-operation boundaries (payment authorization, account changes, data export, privileged actions, key material access) are declared in security_manifest. The blueprint schema does not declare a discrete "sensitive_operations" field at the technical layer because sensitive-operation boundaries are part of security_manifest's per-app declaration rather than a fixed schema enumeration; the applicability filter at tier 3+ with auth_flow != "none" catches the universe of apps for which AUTH-3 is meaningful. Apps with no authentication (technical.auth_flow == "none") are formally inapplicable. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 3+ has sensitive operations beyond initial login because payment, account-change, data-export, or privileged-action surfaces are baseline at Tier 3+.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 4's SEC-PILLAR-4-STEP-UP-AUTH trace was added in v0.5 specifically to provide a 1:1 mapping to MASVS-AUTH-3. The trace requires that sensitive operations (payment authorization, account changes, data export, privileged actions, key material access) require re-authentication or an elevated authentication step beyond initial session login, with the step-up mechanism and trigger boundaries declared in security_manifest. This directly implements MASVS-AUTH-3's requirement that the app secure sensitive operations with additional authentication. Implementation correctness for the chosen mechanism (biometric re-prompt cryptographically bound to the operation, MFA code validation, signed deep-link verification, replay-resistance, sensitive-operation boundary enforcement) requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control state the same requirement, but the auditor evaluation chain (file_standards_auditor + reaper-auth + reaper-mobile + reaper-secrets) is necessary for full evaluation of correctness.

expected_evidence_patterns: security_manifest declares the set of sensitive operations and the step-up mechanism gating each (e.g., {operation: "transfer_funds", step_up: "biometric_with_cryptoobject"}, {operation: "export_data", step_up: "mfa_totp"})

Biometric step-up uses platform frameworks with CryptoObject binding on Android (BiometricPrompt.authenticate(CryptoObject)) or Keychain access-control-flagged keys on iOS — biometric success cryptographically gates the operation, not just a boolean

MFA implementations follow RFC 6238 (TOTP) or RFC 4226 (HOTP) for time-based / counter-based codes — not custom code generation; TOTP secrets stored in platform secure storage (Keychain on iOS, Keystore-derived material on Android)
Email-confirmation or signed-deep-link step-up flows use server-issued, single-use, time-limited tokens with HMAC signature; deep-link receivers validate signature and replay-window before honoring

Step-up gate enforced on the server side at the sensitive-operation endpoint — client-side step-up alone is insufficient because tampered clients can bypass; receipt of step-up credential is server-validated
Step-up failure modes declared: lockout after N failures, escalation path (require remote re-authentication), audit-log emission on sensitive-operation attempts
failure_indicators: Sensitive operation gated only by a client-side boolean flag (e.g., isAuthenticated = true) without server-side step-up validation — vulnerable to tampered-binary bypass per RESILIENCE controls
Biometric step-up prompt that returns success without CryptoObject binding — the success result is a boolean that can be spoofed at runtime on rooted/jailbroken devices
MFA secrets stored in UserDefaults / SharedPreferences plaintext, or transmitted in URL query parameters (visible in referrer headers, server logs)
Step-up token reuse: server accepts the same TOTP code or signed deep-link token for multiple sensitive operations within its validity window (replay vulnerability)
Step-up bypass via UI navigation — e.g., navigating to the sensitive-operation screen via a deep link skips the step-up prompt that gates the normal navigation path
Step-up timeout too long (e.g., one-time step-up valid for entire session) — defeats the purpose of step-up by collapsing it back to initial session authentication
No lockout on step-up retries — unlimited attempts allow brute-force enumeration of MFA codes or local PIN
Step-up implementation in raw WebView with JavaScript bridges (loses platform protection; token interception surface)
false_positive_traps: Step-up handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test mocks legitimately stub MFA prompts and biometric re-authentication; these are not production violations.

Short-lived session caching of step-up credentials (e.g., "biometric verified within last 60 seconds") is NOT a violation if the cache duration is explicitly bounded in security_manifest and the cache invalidates on sensitive-operation parameter change. The violation surface is unbounded caching, not bounded short-window caching.

Apps that intentionally have no "sensitive operations" beyond initial login (e.g., read-only content consumption apps with no transaction surface) are not MASVS-AUTH-3 violations — the control applies when sensitive operations exist. security_manifest must declare the empty set explicitly.

Server-side step-up that is silent to the client (e.g., backend issues a risk-score-driven challenge transparently) is NOT a violation as long as the user experience is meaningful and the challenge is enforced — the form of additional authentication is not prescribed by MASVS-AUTH-3.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 4 step-up authentication (SEC-PILLAR-4-STEP-UP-AUTH) as primary evidence toward MASVS-AUTH-3 coverage
declares sensitive-operation boundaries and step-up mechanisms in security_manifest per MASVS-AUTH-3 platform-best-practice requirement
cryptographically binds biometric step-up success to the gated operation via platform CryptoObject (Android) or access-control-flagged Keychain keys (iOS)
softened_claims: aligned with MASVS-AUTH-3 sensitive-operation authentication guidance
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-AUTH-3. MASVS-AUTH-3 is mapped to one Constitution v0.5 trace in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-4-STEP-UP-AUTH (primary, direct). The trace was added in v0.5 specifically to provide a 1:1 mapping to AUTH-3, so the mapping is direct rather than partial. Claims about step-up security depend on the chosen mechanism — biometric step-up claims require CryptoObject binding (Android) or access-control-flagged Keychain (iOS); MFA claims require RFC-compliant TOTP/HOTP and secure secret storage; signed-deep-link claims require server-issued single-use signed tokens with replay-window enforcement. Server-side step-up enforcement is required for credibility; client-side-only step-up is forbidden by claim boundary because it is bypassable per RESILIENCE controls. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
